ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a generic pizza class (FOODON_00002572). No specific class exists for paneer kebab pizza variant; closest_parent match used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A00LC covers pizza as a broad category. No specific code exists for Indian-style paneer kebab pizza; closest_parent match used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for this specific pizza variant. SNOMED CT coverage for prepared ethnic fusion dishes is limited.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving is approximately 150 g, equivalent to two slices of a standard thin-crust paneer kebab pizza.

nutrient_source.title: Kebab Pizza Paneer — Singapore HPB Foodo Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Kebab Pizza Paneer (cr_id G00010654, confidence 0.95). All per-100g values preserved from the canonical HPB record. Saturated fat estimated at approximately 11.5 g/100g based on typical paneer and mozzarella composition from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (paneer: ~14 g sat fat/100g; mozzarella: ~10 g sat fat/100g) weighted by dish proportion, as HPB does not report saturated fat separately for this item.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for paneer kebab thin-crust pizza. GI estimated at 60 (medium) based on the closest analogous food: thin-crust pizza with cheese and vegetable toppings, GI approximately 60 per University of Sydney GI Database (Atkinson et al., 2008, Diabetes Care). The very low carbohydrate content (5.24 g/100g) means the glycemic load per 150 g serving is low at approximately 4.7 g (GL = GI/100 × carbohydrate_g_per_serving = 60/100 × 7.86 = 4.7). The high fat and protein content from paneer and mozzarella further attenuates the glycaemic response.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 1080 mg/100g — far exceeds the 600 mg/100g upper threshold for hypertension-safe foods
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid or strictly limit this dish; sodium at 1080 mg/100g is nearly double the 600 mg/100g safety threshold, and a 150 g serving delivers approximately 1620 mg sodium, representing over 70% of the recommended daily sodium limit of 2000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low net carbohydrate (5.24 g/100g) but very high fat (26.4 g/100g) and high sodium (1080 mg/100g) require careful portion management
type2_diabetes.guidance: The low carbohydrate content (5.24 g/100g) and estimated medium GI (~60) result in a low glycaemic load per serving (~4.7), which is favourable for blood glucose control; however, the very high fat content (26.4 g/100g, predominantly saturated) and high sodium (1080 mg/100g) pose cardiovascular and renal risks common in Type 2 Diabetes, so portion size should be limited to one to two slices and frequency kept occasional.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High saturated fat (~11.5 g/100g), total fat (26.4 g/100g), sodium (1080 mg/100g), and cholesterol (85 mg/100g) collectively elevate cardiovascular risk
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: This dish is high risk for individuals with cardiovascular disease due to its combination of elevated saturated fat (~11.5 g/100g), total fat (26.4 g/100g), cholesterol (85 mg/100g), and sodium (1080 mg/100g); consumption should be infrequent, portions minimised, and lower-fat cheese substitutions requested where possible.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Saturated fat (~11.5 g/100g) exceeds the 5 g/100g RA threshold; nightshade vegetables (tomato, capsicum) may exacerbate symptoms in sensitive individuals
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should limit this dish due to high saturated fat (~11.5 g/100g), trace trans fat (0.2 g/100g), high sodium (1080 mg/100g), and the presence of nightshade ingredients (tomato, capsicum) which may worsen inflammation in susceptible individuals.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Kebab Pizza with Paneer is an Indian-fusion thin-crust pizza popular in Singapore, featuring paneer kebab (Indian fresh cottage cheese, marinated and grilled) as the primary topping, accompanied by red onion, tomato, capsicum (bell pepper), and pizza sauce on a thin wheat-flour base, typically finished with mozzarella cheese. The dish is fully vegetarian. The thin crust delivers a crisp, light base, while the paneer provides a dense, milky, mildly spiced protein component. The overall flavour profile is savoury and moderately spiced, with the sweetness of capsicum and tomato balancing the richness of the cheese. Nutritionally, this pizza is atypical: the per-100g carbohydrate content is very low at 5.24 g, while fat is very high at 26.4 g (predominantly from paneer and mozzarella, with estimated saturated fat ~11.5 g/100g) and protein is moderate at 10.7 g. Sodium is very high at 1080 mg/100g, making this dish unsuitable for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease without significant modification. The glycaemic load per serving is low (~4.7) due to the minimal carbohydrate content, which is relatively favourable for blood glucose management in Type 2 Diabetes, though the high saturated fat and sodium remain concerns. Calcium is notable at 118 mg/100g from the dairy components. This dish is classified as processed, allergen-relevant for gluten and dairy, and is not suitable for vegan or gluten-free diets.
common_user_queries: Is paneer pizza good for diabetics?
Is paneer pizza suitable for hypertension?

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo canonical database entry for this exact dish (cr_id G00010654, confidence 0.95). Saturated fat estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy component data for paneer and mozzarella, clearly documented. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for thin-crust cheese and vegetable pizza, appropriately disclosed.
notes: dietary_fibre_g is reported as 0.0 by HPB for this dish; this is a confirmed zero per the HPB source, consistent with a thin-crust pizza with minimal vegetable fibre contribution at the reported serving composition. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, water_g, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions, micronutrient panel, and moisture data for this specific paneer kebab pizza preparation are not available in the HPB Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central as a composite dish entry as of 2026-04-16. trans_fat_g set to 0.2 g/100g based on typical trace trans fat content in commercial mozzarella and paneer from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy; this is an estimate and should be verified if a full fatty acid panel becomes available. saturated_fat_g set to 11.5 g/100g: estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy paneer and mozzarella component data weighted by typical dish proportion; HPB does not report saturated fat separately for this item. GI of 60 is an analogous estimate for thin-crust cheese and vegetable pizza from the University of Sydney GI Database (Atkinson et al., 2008); no direct GI measurement for paneer kebab pizza exists in the approved GI literature. Glycaemic load per 150 g serving calculated as: GL = (60/100) × (5.24 × 1.5) = 4.7.
